questions.question: Explain the three requirements that must be satisfied by any solution to the Critical Section problem.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can be in critical section at a time
Progress: If no process is in critical section and some want to enter, one must be selected without delay
Bounded Waiting: There is a limit on number of times other processes can enter CS after a process requests entry

questions.question: Differentiate between binary semaphore and counting semaphore with an example.
keyPoints: Binary semaphore: Takes only 0 and 1, used for mutual exclusion (like a lock)
Counting semaphore: Takes integer values >1, controls access to multiple identical resources (like 5 identical resources)
Example: Binary = single bathroom lock, Counting = 5 identical printers

questions.question: What is busy waiting? Explain one advantage and one disadvantage of busy waiting.
keyPoints: Busy waiting: Process repeatedly checks a condition in a loop without blocking
Advantage: No context switch overhead - useful for short critical sections
Disadvantage: Wastes CPU cycles that could be used by other processes

questions.question: Explain the Producer-Consumer problem and discuss why synchronization is needed.
keyPoints: Producer creates items and places in bounded buffer, Consumer removes items
Synchronization needed to prevent: Producer overwriting unfetched items (overflow)
Also prevents: Consumer accessing empty buffer (underflow)
Buffer has finite size requiring coordination between processes

questions.question: What is the difference between a mutex and a semaphore?
keyPoints: Mutex provides mutual exclusion with ownership concept - only lock owner can unlock
Semaphore does not have ownership - any process can signal
Mutex is binary (0/1), semaphore can be counting
Mutex ensures single access, semaphore can allow multiple concurrent accesses

questions.question: Describe the Readers-Writers problem and state why multiple readers can access data simultaneously.
keyPoints: Readers-Writers: Multiple readers can read shared data but writers need exclusive access
Multiple readers allowed because reading does not modify data - no inconsistency
Writers modify data and need exclusive access to prevent partial/inconsistent updates
Various solutions: readers-preference, writers-preference, fair

questions.question: Explain the role of TestAndSet instruction in process synchronization.
keyPoints: TestAndSet is a hardware atomic instruction
Atomically reads current value of lock variable and sets it to true
Returns previous value - allows implementing mutual exclusion
Used to implement spinlocks in multiprocessor systems
Ensures atomicity without software race conditions

questions.question: Explain Peterson's solution for two processes. Show how it satisfies all three requirements of the Critical Section problem with necessary data structures and algorithm.
keyPoints: Data structures: int turn; boolean flag[2]
Algorithm: Before entering CS, process sets flag true, sets turn to other, then waits while other process wants in AND it's other's turn
Mutual Exclusion: Both cannot be in CS simultaneously - flag and turn ensure this
Progress: If other doesn't want CS or it's our turn, we proceed
Bounded Waiting: Each process can be overtaken at most once before entering
Pseudo-code explanation with wait loop

questions.question: Discuss the Dining Philosophers problem. Explain how deadlock can occur and describe one solution to prevent deadlock.
keyPoints: Five philosophers alternate between thinking and eating, need two forks to eat
Deadlock occurs if all pick up left fork simultaneously or follow same pattern - circular wait
Solution 1: Asymmetric - odd philosophers pick left then right, even pick right then left
Solution 2: Allow only 4 philosophers at table
Solution 3: Use a waiter (semaphore) to control access
Each solution breaks one of the four deadlock conditions

questions.question: Explain semaphore operations wait() and signal(). Show how semaphores can be used to solve the Critical Section problem.
keyPoints: wait() decrements semaphore, blocks if value becomes negative
signal() increments semaphore, wakes waiting process if any
Initial value determines number of concurrent accesses (1 for mutual exclusion
For CS: Initialize semaphore to 1, wait() before CS, signal() after CS
Implementation can be with queues to avoid busy waiting
Works for both uniprocessor and multiprocessor systems

questions.question: What is a monitor? Explain how monitors achieve process synchronization with an example.
keyPoints: Monitor is high-level synchronization construct
Encapsulates shared variables and procedures in one unit
Automatically enforces mutual exclusion - only one process can execute inside at a time
Condition variables allow processes to wait inside monitor
Example: monitor Buffer { int count=0; condition full, empty;
producer() waits on empty, consumer() waits on full; automatic CS enforcement
